name: S1 27.6 - Rio Grande Ford
details: Mandatory unbridged crossing of Rio Grande within White Rock Canyon. Suggested fording location is at bottom of Frijoles Overlook Trail, at a wide-ish spot where river can be up to 300ft across (but shallower and with less current than elsewhere esp upstream). River here is under influence from Cochiti Reservoir downstream. Muddy banks common. Avoid during times of high run-off (CW consider beginning hike in White Rock instead of SF). Check USGS streamflow gage reports for Otowi Bridge (along NM 502 too far / illegal to walk upstream) before starting from Santa Fe. Swimming or an inflatable raft / kayak have historically been required during avg. May / June flow. WB turn around if unsafe! Return toward SF, then RTD bus to White Rock and backtrack via WR Alt Rte to other side of Rio to connect footsteps minus the crossing. (CCW can also use Alt to do same in reverse.)

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Forded just south of waypoint. Fairly swift and up to bellybutton of 6'6" man. Probably wouldn't do again.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: Looks like 900-1000 cfs at nearest stream gage (Otowi), which is near average for the date. Sounds like most hikers will still want a means of floating across at this location on a late summer or autumn hike (avg cfs comes down to 600-700 by late September).

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: Assume a "tall person fording" would mean a smaller person could safely swim at these flows if needed.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
5/2/23 Swam the river at over 4000 CFS, which was definitely a true start to this adventure. Here was my strategy. 1) Seal as much as would fit in my pack liner by twisting, folding over, and using some cord to clove hitch this inner bladder (ensure it holds air when you press on it). 2) Tie a ~10' length of paracord to the pack and place the entire pack into a 55 gal contractor garbage bag. Push out excess air and twist the bag opening such that cord comes out of the end. 3) Use the cord to tie off the bag opening ensuring that it holds air. 4) Use the additional cord to make a large tether with a loop that you can slip very easily over your shoulder (I made a double bowline with large loops for each shoulder). 5) Swim like your life depends on it with the pack floating behind you.
This worked well for me, but here are some surprises that I had. The river was so high that it was well over the bank and it required significant wading through the flooded brush to get to the main channel. Thankfully this crossing point is mostly thorn free, but my contractor bag still got one small hole poked in it by this brush. I rotated this hole to be above the water before the actual crossing, but this is why the inner pack liner needs to be your backup floatation. The force of this water was amazing I could only walk about a 1/3 of the way across before needing to swim clear to the other bank. Swimming was a relief as I wasn't walking so much as sliding on the bottom at a steep angle. I approached this crossing being prepared to turn back if I wasn't confident in my plan, with my inreach mini secured around my neck, and mentally prepared to lose my backpack if my life depended on it.  
Ultimately I landed safely on the other side 200-300 ft downstream pumped that I had done this and I would do it again. 
Please make your own decision, but since no one can say for sure what the channel will do over time I would recommend you always approach this crossing prepared to swim or with some other plan than just wading.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
I managed to get drinking water from the Rio Grande by adding some PAC Flocculant in an eye dropper to my water kit. Look up "Water Wizard for Rafters" and do the math for your own eye dropper. In 20 minutes the silt had almost completely settled out of a 2L hanging CNOC bladder and after discarding the first 0.5L with the floc in it, I then filtered and purified the remaining 1.5L.
I did this as I took the S1A shortcut up the canyon, which was very steep and required some scrambling but was definitely worth it. Just go slow and steady.

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Hip to knee deep and not that cold with warm weather this week. (CCW)

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Forded at 700 cfs. Definitely a difficult ford but was doable without a float. Would recommend one. Over my belly button (I'm about 6'ish.) If I was any shorter or the flow was any higher I'd want a float but it was doable without for me

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: 5/28/2025 It's really difficult to get to the water because of the vegetarion. Fording was difficult because of murky bottom - never knew if I would have ground under my feet. There were sandbanks to give me a break but deeper swift channel close to West Bank.  Wouldn't have made it without a floaty - used my Thermorest Neo stuck between my back and pack. I made it by dumm luck or divine intervention however one wants look at it. Think carefully about it if your not a tall strong dude or a very strong swimmer.

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Managed to ford this on a 590 CFS day. Water came up to my middle (I’m 5’11’’) you could actually see the sandbanks the water was that low.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Took the XC alt, CCW, to get down, which worked OK, minor scrambling. Lucked out with only about 350 cfs. Still used the trash bag and tether method Will described above. Mostly below the waist except for a brief moment when I swam, channel was deep right away on the west side. Plenty of Sand bars. On the other side, willows and brush were awful… CCW, try to cross where brush looks less intense if you can. Saw bear prints in the mud on both sides, guess it’s not just NNML hikers crossing here!

name: S1 28.2 - W1 (XC>TR)
details: Seasonal water in Frijoles Canyon here, flowing through its alluvial fan near confluence with Rio Grande. XC ends hereabouts. Semi-informal River Trail continues NE, again near banks of Rio, back from riverside brush.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text:
No flow but puddle at or near waypoint. No silt versus Rio Grande. 

Excellent water further on main route. Multiple springs flowing at mouth of Chaquehui Canyon.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 5/2/23 There was definitely water flowing here as I could see both water falls flowing from the east side of rio grande on the way down.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Dry but there's flow in Ancho Canyon in a couple miles

name: S1 30.7 - w2 (S1B START)
details: Seasonal water where River Trail crosses Ancho Canyon. Good alternative to drinking from the Rio Grande. Just beyond is jct w/ Ancho Rapids Tr, which main route follows N soon climbing out of White Rock Cyn on established trail. White Rock Resupply Loop instead stays with River Trail, which itself continues to improve heading NE, then joins other established trails up to village of White Rock. Very scenic albeit considerably longer than main route - only really recommended if intending to resupply or to send home a packraft after fording Rio.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Flowing. Not a ton so idk how long it'll last but there was plenty for my needs. Flow is stronger slightly uphill before the trail exits the drainage

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: 5/28/2025 still flowing, especially a little upstream

name: S1 38.6 - TH CG W2 (RD>TR)
details: Trailhead for Frey Trail in Juniper Campground. Should be developed water here (along with toilets etc).

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Bottle filler on at TH, heated restrooms with water (no showers), and utility sink.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Water faucet right off trail in the campground

name: S1 40.8 - HQ W3
details: Bandelier NM visitor center / permit office. Get free overnight permit to spend more time in the Bandelier backcountry. This is only place to get a permit to be legal overnight within the park. (And you'll find that A] staying overnight makes taking the main rather roundabout / worthwhile route in the park much easier, while B] the Frijoles Canyon Alt may speed you through the park and avoid the need to camp, which is good since you can't camp in canyon bottoms anyway). You should also pay the entrance fee while at the VC ($15 per indv arriving on foot., good for a week). Shuttles stop in the parking lot seasonally and run to village of White Rock, providing an alternative way to resupply vs walking into town via the Resupply Loop - useful if you took the Frijoles RIM Alt into the park to get here).

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Bandelier now encourages you to get a backcountry permit 24 hours in advance of your arrival. This is new for 2024. Details on their website https://www.nps.gov/band/planyourvisit/backpacking.htm

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
Excellent Bottle Filler (to the right of the visitor center entrance) and bathrooms, both of which appear to be available 24hrs.
There appears to be a restaurant onsite as well, but I don't have any info on it.

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: CCW, can pay entry fee online but cannot get backcountry camping permit without going to VC in person because of required bear resistant container rules. Cafe and gift shop open and bottle filler on.

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: There is no more bus to White Rock. The shuttle just goes visitor center to campground.

name: S1 64.0 - W2-3
details: E Fork Jemez River. First crossing (usu a small creek). Becomes reliable down-canyon.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 5/3/23 High spring flow lead to this portion of the hike being gorgeous, but wetter and more interesting than I had planned for. No swimming this time, but there were well over 20 crossings over the entire length of this hike until the Las Conchas day use area, some of which were 3ft deep and pretty swift flowing. Significant portions of the side trail were also flooded with 6-12 inches of water and my footwear selection could have been better for what was quite a bit of time being wet and also hiking over boulders at points.  Still with all this water there were lots of places to camp once you pass back into SFNF.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Wide and a good flow. Plenty of opportunities to access water in the next three miles between here and waypoint 67.0. More access after 67.0 but it is a very popular trail there with lots of people, dogs, and babies. So upstream of here is likely cleaner water.

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Plenty of flow all along the creek

sheetComments.author: Drew Smith
sheetComments.text: Slow and turbid but plenty of water. Tastes fine.

sheetComments.author: John W

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 5/2/23 Confirming that the water spigot just up the hill from this was running, although I think this campground only opens 15 May

sheetComments.author: Max Bliss
sheetComments.text: Water spigot on and delicious. River is flowing great as well.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text:
Water spigot up the hill is NOT on. Probably will be before long but 🤷‍♀️. River is flowing well. 

Also, shortly after here CW when the route departs trail 137, there is a decent size logging operation going on. I didn't run into any problems because it was later in the evening but that could be an issue. Might be better to follow trail 137 to the end (getting to see the warm springs and Battleship Rock) and then road walk the 3 or 4 miles along the highway into town.

sheetComments.author: John W
sheetComments.text: Confirming spigot not on. River was flowing.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Spigot on at day use area, near restrooms. Also the river! Agree with comment that before this, CCW, it’s a lot of walking in an active logging area. No problem on a Sunday but may impact weekdays

name: S2 3.8 - W3 JL

name: S2 6.7 - W3 TH JR
details: Trail crosses Rio San Antonio. CW N from trailhead either along creekside meadow trail(s) or closed road above creek.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Don't miss the san antonio hot springs up the hill, they have one of the best views around and hopefully don't get too crowded.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Good flow. Hot springs were nice, just take the trail uphill from the east side of the bridge. A few folks were there early Sunday morning but they were on their way out. Quite a few pools.

sheetComments.author: Drew Smith
sheetComments.text: Good flow, hot springs totally worth the short climb

name: S2 28.6 - W2
details: seasonal flow

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Dry, but two meadows north, water flowing across trail

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text:
Completely dry. 
Blowdowns East of here were a pain in the @$$. If I were to do it again I would try staying on FR70 to Palomas Trail.
Ahead look for wooden posts (4ft tall, 8" around, pointed tops) to guide you through the meadows. Beware of multiple herd paths that look more like a trail than the actual trail or you'll end up doing a lot of course correction. 
Came across a trail crew working on Penas Negras West of the Penchas trail junction. They have a lot of work ahead of them but maybe by next year this trail will be manageable

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Dry. Only about half a mile of blowdowns left and now marked surprisingly well with ribbon for easy navigation. Otherwise, wooden posts, i-blazes, cut logs, and cairns lead the way.

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: There was a small creek running at about 28.8. As far as the blow down area goes I thought it was bad from about 27.4 to 28.6. I did see some pink ribbons but a lot of them are gone.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Dry. Still a lot of blowdowns to manage.

sheetComments.author: Drew Smith
sheetComments.text: Looks like the FS has been all the way through, but trees continue to fall and block the route. Don’t plan on a pace better than 1 mph through here

name: S2 30.0 - W2

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Source after 30.9 flowing again. Most meadows are full of water. See a lot of pools passing by.

sheetComments.author: Brett T

sheetComments.author: Andreas

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe

I would've drank from at least three sources on the CDT that were described as "useless" and the like. Sobos just hitting NM still think in terms of good and bad and nothing in between.

name: S2 41.5 - W2
details: Cecilia Canyon

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text:
Good flow. Clear and cold.

There were at least two flowing, unlisted sources between here and 33.8.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Cecilia Canyon water was somewhat silty with spring runoff. Several good water sources between here and San Pedro Parks Wilderness, specifically there was an excellent spring just off trail at 36.177520, -106.797299 with good camping nearby along the old road.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Good strong flowing spring just off trail at 40.1

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: The trail is closed ahead at 46.8/Hwy 96 to Skull Bridge due to the Indios fire. Coyote Ranger station is 1.5 down the road to the east. In addition to water and camping, Coyote Ranger station is also offering a shuttle that runs at 7 am to either the Abiquiu Dam or Ghost Ranch. They also have filtered water inside and flush toilets during open hours. There are porta potties with hand wash stations in the parking lot. They have a picnic table across from the entrance with some shade, plus water jugs and a table full of snacks, fruit, and hand sanitizer. Camping is anywhere behind the brown fence.

name: S2 51.0 - W2 0.35E

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer

sheetComments.author: Drew Smith

sheetComments.author: John W
sheetComments.text: Lots of water most of the way down the canyon. Heavily burned area.

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: RAGING! I went through in a thunderstorm and saw my first flash flood, took some getting around though!

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Nice creek with good flow in spite of the burn. Lots of fire in this area over recent decades.

sheetComments.author: Drew Smith
sheetComments.text: Low but flowing, less silty farther down

name: S2 60.8 - W1 100ft NW
details: A trough (full and flowing) was present 6-2017 100 ft NW of route in sagebrush meadow.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: 9/21 This source might not be producing currently but there are at least 3 other active troughs in the middle of valley approaching Rio Chama

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Couldn't locate trough at waypoint. Piped spring to trough roughly .3 SW just off trail was flowing good.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Trough 0.5 miles from Rio Chama wasn't flowing in Spring 2023, but trough ~1.1 miles before the chama had excellent water. Trough at 36.31882, -106.64468

sheetComments.author: John W
sheetComments.text: Saw 1 trough from the trail, had plenty of water.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Best water around, other stuff was super muddy

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Nothing at waypt but trough described as 0.3 mi SW, very close to trail, was good; trough full of algae but around inflow could get good water. The other trough, with coordinates mentioned above, about a mile further SW, also close to trail, was similar; semi-hidden by vegetation.

name: S2 60.9 - W3
details: Rio Chama / Skull Bridge. Footbridge over renowned whitewater river. Questionable water quality though.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: I've always seen this flowing, but it's always been silty/muddy

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Once again, I passed this after heavy rain it was flying through. Wouldn’t want to have drank any though, but, when needs must.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Lots of silty water! Nice spot to cool off under the bridge though

sheetComments.author: Drew Smith
sheetComments.text: Low water (for rafting )but fairly clear, not chocolaty as it sometimes is

name: S2 74.4 - W2
details: Suspension bridge over Canjilon Creek.

name: S3 1.1 - W1
details: Arroyo del Yeso seasonal flow

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sep 2023. Good Flow through ~ mile 2 when you exit the canyon.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text:
Light flow. Enough to collect but probably not for much longer. There is better flow upstream. 

Past here the NNML track varies from the CDT Ghost Ranch Alternate. The Alt is more obvious, more well worn, and marked with blue blazes on trees and an occasional cairn. I suspect there might have been some rerouting and the two might have been previously aligned. I stayed with the Alt to Yeso Tank.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Lots more water upstream along trail. Very scenic.

name: S3 6.9 - W1 JL
details: Yeso Tank (often foul). CW follow berm N to road W.

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: 10/21 indeed foul, but would do in a pinch.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Very muddy, but I successfully pulled 2L that I used flocculant on before filtering.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text:
The tank is fenced in and populated with cows. If you took the CDT Alt there is Dead End Tank before this that is easier to access. 

Past here the CDT Alt goes northeast in front of the tank. The NNML heads north to the left of the tank. The NNML North of here has CDT markers so again I suspect some rerouting has occurred. The NNML will bring you to another water source and gets you to the CDT proper faster.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Not the worst tank seen in the trail. Plenty of water.

name: S3 15.1 - W2-3 100yds E
details: Harris Bear Spring troughs located along S side of corral fence 50-100 yds E of trail.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Great water at upper trough, don't get disheartened when you first see the empty lower trough.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Upper trough is filled with a pipe and over flowing

name: S3 28.0 - W3
details: Canjilon Creek crossing. Should be reliable here.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Strong flow. That's what a river should look like. There's also water flowing across trail in a couple spots a mile to a mile and a half before if in dire need and can't wait

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: From mile 27.1 to 72 there is water at all indicated locations and plenty in between. Problem is not to find water but to keep it out of your shoes.

name: S3 30.0 - W1
details: Canjilon Creek (unreliable to N)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Acceptable Flow at multiple points between here and mile 28.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Lots of water before and after here

name: S3 36.1 - W1
details: Ojito Azul. Seasonal flow possible in drainage here.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Good Scoopable Water that you can reach in the upturned culvert. Sep 23

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Good flow but further downhill from waypoint past cattle guard

name: S3 36.7 - W2
details: Canada Jarosita crossing

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Light flow the previous one at 36.1 is better and easier to collect from

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Dry right at crossing, pools just downstream. Also, there is a spring and trough just a little ways east of here. A bit banged up from a fallen tree, down slope from trail, easy to see, and it looked like it had water.

name: S3 41.9 - W3
details: footbridge over Rio Vallecitos. (Bridge wiped out in flood 2017, hopefully since replaced. Can usually ford but use care in finding a safe location.)

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: 10/21 no bridge, but signs pointing the way to a precarious log crossing. Creative rock hop opportunities as well.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: While high snowmelt caused significant issues crossing this in May 2023, this was an easy rock hop in Sept 2023.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: No bridge. Sketchy right now because it's so full of snow melt. Waist high, fast, and cold with uneven slippery boulders as footing. Be careful!

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: Shin deep. Someone just rock hopped it

name: S3 44.3 - W2
details: Rock Creek crossing

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Some flow and pools, scooping helped, good quality

name: S3 52.2 - W2
details: Roadside trough along Hopewell Campground access road / CDT, full 6- 2017. Campground proper (E) reportedly also has faucets and vault toilets, likely seasonal.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 2023. Water is off in the campground, but in addition to the lake the campground host told me that he had some water available for hikers and bikers.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Faucets off and pit toilets closed

sheetComments.author: Dagmar Growe
sheetComments.text: Toilets open., but no water at camp ground. Trough is unfilterable! (I think it's blood in there). Go to lake .

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Toilets open. Faucet not working and trough is nearly empty

name: S3 52.6 - W3
details: Hopewell Lake with water from lake or adjacent facilities.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Faucets are off but CG host at site 4 has water for hikers.

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman
sheetComments.text: Faucets still off but campground host offered to fill all of our bottles with potable water. He said there's an algae bloom in the lake, so best not to drink from it.

name: S3 58.7 - W1
details: Jawbone Tank (stock pond)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Full, but muddy. Flocculant used to pull 2L. Sept 23

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Plenty of water and snow melt flowing in

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Probably better than the previous 2 sources CCW

name: S3 60.8 - W1-2
details: Canon Manga (seasonal)

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Lots of pools of water around

name: S3 61.3 - W1-2
details: Spring Tank (stock pond)

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Full and the frogs are singing

name: S3 66.4 - W3 !!
details: Rio San Antonio crossing. Somewhat confusing how the trail crosses the creek, though it does about what the GPS track here shows (CW climbing steeply up the ridge to the north after crossing).

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Flowing well though smaller than I imagined. I'm cutting off section 3 (and the Chama resupply) and following Rio San Antonio to pick up section 4.

name: S3 69.7 - W1
details: Lagunitas Creek headwaters

name: S3 72.0 - C W3
details: Lagunitas Campground. Perennial water from area lakes / ponds.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 23. Lots of water along with people fishing and hunting camps

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Plenty of water and some good spots to camp.

name: S3/4 78.0 - END Sec 3 (W0)
details: Brazos Ridge Overlook; end Sec.3 at Sec 3A (to N) and Sec 4 (to E). Most hikers will probably want to resupply in Chama by continuing north on the CDT 17 mi to Cumbres Pass CO, then hitchhiking 12 miles SW into this full-service town. Section 4 begins here (SE), after returning from Chama either via repeat of S3A alternate southbound or (when train from Chama to Osier is running), S4A southbound.

sheetComments.author: Hannah Meduna
sheetComments.text: If you need water, along road 87 to the west there are 4+ good streams between here and where it connects to the cdt in 3.5 miles

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Alternatively if you are section hiking and have car access, I left myself a food cache (bear bag hang) at Hopewell Lake and a water cache at 285 to successfully do a combined S3&4. Although Chama is lovely

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Friendly reminder if using FarOut to supplement water info, take comments with a grain of salt. I would've drank from at least three sources on the CDT that were described as "useless" and the like. Sobos just hitting NM still think in terms of good and bad and nothing in between.

name: S4 3.2 - W3
details: Beaver Creek accessible nearby for several miles.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Flowing good. Flow starts about .5 miles from ridgeline.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 23. Lots of great wter here from first Beaver Creek crossing ~1mi from start of section

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Lots of water through this section til mile 24 when you leave Rio San Antonio for the last time

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Beaver Creek flowing all the way along

name: S4 4.4 - W3
details: Diablo Creek accessible nearby for several miles.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: Thanks Paul! Hey, don’t mark your own comments as Resolved or they’ll be hidden from view. I’ve made that not very obvious mistake myself! :)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 23. Tons of water here as well

name: S4 6.6 - W2
details: Fork of Diablo Creek. Less reliable upstream.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 23. Good flow up into meadow ahead.

name: S4 10.3 - W2
details: Canon Largo

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 23. Good Flow from where you first enter creek at ~ mile 9 and onward

sheetComments.author: Hannah Meduna
sheetComments.text: Flowing from here downstream to junction with Rio San Antonio. Within .4 upstream, it dissipates and there’s just occasional muddy puddles.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Flow begins just a few hundred yards from North Largo Tank.

name: S4 11.5 - W3
details: Canon Largo at Rio San Antonio. Continue on use-trails along/near drainage bottom.

name: S4 15.7 - W3
details: Rio San Antonio nearby. CW last access on public land for 2-3 mi

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Sept 23. Cold and Clear. Recommend pulling water here rather than at the road crossing ahead

name: S4 19.5 - JL (W3 0.1S)
details: FR 87 at FR 78. Rio San Antonio access 0.1S via FR 87.

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Flowing well. Great shade under some Cottonwoods just before.

sheetComments.author: Hannah Meduna
sheetComments.text: Flowing, but worse quality water than the upstream or downstream options. There’s currently a beaver dam directly upstream.

name: S4 23.6 - W2
details: CW reach Rio San Antonio in canyon bottom.

name: S4 24.3 - JR W2
details: CW x-c exit canyon bottom E. Last access Rio San Antonio. CW water warning: in dry times this may be your last likely water for 21 mi, or sure-bet until the Rio Grande in 29 mi. (CCW Rio San Antonio first access here although somewhat less reliable than several miles upstream where next approached.)

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: 10/21 Rio San Antonio flowing throughout canyon

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
Sept 23 flowing well throughout canyon.
Re-opened

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Water flowing good through here. There is water ahead on SA

name: S4 27.8 - W1-2 100ft S
details: San Antonio Mtn Tank (seasonal). More likely than Lower San Antone Tank to SE but still unreliable during dry times. Don't count on finding water anywhere on San Antonio Mountain.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 20 Sept 2023. Muddy, but a decent amount of water

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Half green, half brown, but pretty full still.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Not great water but there is a little water

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Chocolate milk, and some algae, but it is water

name: S4 28.4 - W1
details: Lower San Antone Tank (less reliable)

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: I went XC to true summit then service road down so I did not pass this one

name: S4 33.8 - JR W1-2 0.25N
details: BLM road junction. Pass through fence / cattleguard. W side of fence, NE along road, 0.25 to Wissmath Crater seasonal stock pond. Not reliable in drier times. Recently dry late June 2020. Also see similar 'crater' pond nearby S of route.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Tank north of waypoint 31.9 is full. Muddy but better than San Antone tank.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 20 Sept 23. North pond appeared to have a small puddle still, although it looks like it could dry up shortly without more rain.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: I left myself a cache of water at hwy 285 before hiking this section to eliminate any water anxiety in this stretch. About 75’ SE of the 285 gate behind the small bush there is some old twisted metal that I used to hide my water. A cache isn’t needed, if you are aggressive at either carrying a lot of water into this stretch or just picking up all the muddy pond water you see and not trusting only in the 7638 well

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: I left a cache at the hwy crossing to be safe. A quarter after the crossing are three metal troughs that appear new. The rancher was filling as I went by.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: South pond is dry, did not check out North pond

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Dry. But there are some tanks ahead CCW before the highway. One had some decent looking water in it just a bit of algae.

name: S4 45.4 - W2
details: 7638 Well' - 4 or 5 full troughs on satellites; historically reliable. Water here for most if not all NNML hikers so far. If happens to be dry, CCW best bet may be to seek out pair of solar troughs 3.5 miles NW on active grazing allotment. Main road that takes you part-way between here and there carries some vehicle traffic in an emergency. (CW another trough exists roughly 3.5 mi ahead and 0.5 off-route, or can drink from Rio Grande in emergency.)

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: 9/13/21: Good. All but one (tire with float valve) is dry.

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: 10/10/21 5 empty troughs and one tire with water.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Tire trough with float valve is full. Good water.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
21 Sept 2023 This site is currently dry except for a small puddle of rainwater in one of the troughs. Maybe 2 liters if you scooped carefully
The stock pond at mile 49.5 is full, which might be why this site was allowed to go dry. The water at 49.5 is very muddy.
At mile 50 there is another full stock pond 0.1 mi east, which also looks muddy

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman
sheetComments.text: Tire is full and float valve is functional for getting good, clear water.

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: 10/03 Full tire with a wide poop algae moat of sadness around it. But probably fine if you're taller than 5'2"?

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: All the troughs I looked in were dry but there were cows nearby so maybe there was water somewhere nearby and I just didn't look hard enough. 🤷‍♀️ Wouldn't count on it

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Tire was full of good water, phew! You can also use the valve on the large water tank. Also, fyi, there was a wildlife tank just west of waypt 51.2 marked as W0-1. It had a small collection area, well fenced. Could work in a pinch.

sheetComments.author: Hannah Meduna
sheetComments.text: Tire full, no algae, float valve works.

name: S4 52.9 - event, you may need to backtrack up to the trailhead at previous
landmark: event, you may need to backtrack up to the trailhead at previous
details: Rio Grande here is typically 50 ft wide, knee deep with a modest current except during times of heavy runoff. Eastbound hikers, in the hopefully unlikely event that you can't wait out a high water event, you may need to backtrack up to the trailhead at previous waypoint and then follow roads south to County Rd B007 / Dunn Bridge crossing of the Rio Grande, 20 miles total, then continue 2 mi farther to the village of Arroyo Hondo at NM Hwy 522.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Crossed downstream of rapids. 180 CFS. Had to swim but current wasn't really noticable.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 21 Sep 23. Water quality looked better than anything seen until the Rio San Antonio. See notes about the 7638 well.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: 21 Sep 23. Crossed upstream of the small rapids at the widest spot where the east side switchbacks start. Only 135 CFS and at this spot I couldn’t feel any current, but ended up still having to swim which was easy to do. In retrospect, you can better see where you can likely just walk across from higher up in the canyon by spotting the darker (less silty) water closer to the rapids to the south (the better crossing point had tiny riffles). This difference wasn’t obvious to see at the water level, as it was from some elevation.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Downsteam gauge was at 4.5 ft and 600 cfs. I crossed at widest point between downstream ripples and upstream ones. Aimed for large boulder where trail comes down on the east side. I had to swim 90% of the width. More difficult than I thought but everything came out unscathed. I'm not a strong swimmer and used my inflatable mattress and pillow as flotation devices for myself and my pack.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Shin deep, 50cfs and 1.25’ I think lower than normal for Sep

name: S4 53.9 - W1 0.25SW
details: Side road SW to circular trough w float valve, visible on satellite

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: A tire tank with decent water, just had to chase the cows away from it

name: S5 2.7 - W3
details: Cabresto Creek first access CW. Easy creek access south side of road by gauging station. (CW Collect here to avoid some private property heading east.)

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Good flow all along the road

name: S5 6.1 - JL (W3 100ydS)
details: FR 134 at FR 134A. CW left on FR 134A. Cabresto Creek access across road. (CCW Collect here to avoid some private property heading west.)

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Good flow all along the road

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Good flow. Please be aware if you are using the new alt route to avoid the blowdowns up near Mount Baldy this is the last water until Red River.

name: S5 8.6 - W3

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Strong flow and plenty of places to access off the trail

name: S5 10.5 - JL W3
details: Lake Fork Tr 82 at Bull Creek Tr 85. (Bull Creek Fork meets Lake Fork Cabresto Creek.) (CW Bad weather route avoiding Latir Mesa continues on Tr 82 NE.)

name: S5 11.0 - W3
details: Cross wide-ish rock-hoppable Bull Fork Cabresto Creek. Regular creek access here and above along trail in canyon.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Flowing, and, CCW, camp spots just before

name: S5 12.4 - W2
details: First / last access Bull Fork Cabresto Creek

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Low and narrow but flowing fast.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Dry at the way point, but lots of water further along (CCW)

name: S5 16.2 - XT W3
details: Heart Lake. Tr 85 crosses outlet near jct connector Tr 115. (CCW Bad weather route avoiding Latir Mesa uses Tr 115 to Tr 82 S.)

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Pretty little lake, outlet had good flow. CCW, Latir Peak /mesa will be as high as you’ll get the rest of the trip. Soak it up!

name: S5 16.8 - W2
details: Actual crossing of low flow muddy Baldy cabin Creek

sheetComments.author: Paul Healy
sheetComments.text: Passed this source without noticing a flow

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
July 2023. Only a trickle.
Caution. Between here and Midnight TH 22.4 (CW) there are four Blowdowns (BD) that will slow your progress and require some XC. Here are the rough locations and my best suggestion for the two worst ones (19.5 & 21.8).
BD 17.2  36.789, -105.4416
BD 19.5  36.781, -105.409  Avoid by diverting XC to the north over the ridgeline and working your way back to the trail.
BD 21.2  36.776, -105.391
BD 21.8  36.779, -105.384  Avoid by going XC to the south and working your way to the forest road and Midnight TH 22.4.

Look for still standing forest and stay in that if possible, the terrain made it difficult to understand the best way past the two large blowdowns and I stupidly climbed into them first before then trying to find a way out of them. Further insights are welcome and needed.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Dry, a little water and mud further down but recommend heart lake instead

name: S5 22.4 - TH JL W2
details: Midnight Tr 81 TH at FR 134. Cabresto Creek across road (somewhat seasonal here). (CCW last water until Baldy Cabin / Heart Lake.)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
July 2023 Good Flow. 
Caution. Between here and Baldy Cabin (CCW) there are four areas of significant blowdown. See the comment on 16.8 for more details

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Flowing nicely here. Called ranger station and blowdowns have not been cleared. Check for private property and slope angle, but water all along 134.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text:
There is an alternate trail and a blowdown bypass trail, so you can basically stick with the Midnight Trail. Here is my track for the alternate trail and notes on it and the bypass trail: https://caltopo.com/m/G11UERS

name: S5 25.0 - W2
details: Bitter Creek

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
Excellent Flow. Also here is some history on the gold mines along this route.
https://www.rrraven.com/pages/roycepeggyfiles/rr%20gold%20mines/web-content/edison.html

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Valle Vidal Alt has water everywhere you expect it to be, some with more cows than others. Longest stretch without would be 2 or 3 miles south of Little Costilla Peak.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Valle Vidal CCW: plenty of water in the creeks once you drop in the valley, for you and the cows. After grassy creek, no water till you start the descent from little costilla. I encountered a spring along the old road you go down, maybe a 5 mi dry stretch. Then another 5mi or so to Comanche creek though the XC drainage you descend could work. Streams en route to main trail as you ascend, too. Route spends a lot of time on abandoned roads and some XC, follow the track carefully so you don’t miss turnoffs.

name: S5 28.3 - W3
details: Bitter Creek above Memphis mine (historic). Likely perennial here.

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: 10/21 Bitter Creek flowing strong throughout canyon

name: S6 1.6 - W2-3 JL
details: Red River Nature Trail end at FR 486 / Goose Creek Trail (4WD road). Placer Creek. CW head E on road.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Flowing good. Helicopters scooping water out of it

name: S6 3.3 - TH JR W3
details: Paved NM 578 at Goose Creek TH. Reliable water in creek along length of trail with frequent access.

sheetComments.author: Kerry Stewart
sheetComments.text: Flowing from here to the lake.

name: S6 6.6 - C W3

sheetComments.text: Campsite no longer exists/covered in blowdowns.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer

name: S6 8.7 - W3
details: Goose Creek last / first access. (Reliable flow throughout canyon)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Caution. Blowdown along Goose Creek Trail starts (CW) at around mile 8,  36.6442, -105.4376 and continues to Goose Lake. At start of blowdown go NW (uphill XC) and join the forest road (36.645, -105.44) for the remainder to Goose Lake.

sheetComments.author: Brian Tanzman
sheetComments.text: Flowing good. Blowdowns have been recently cut and trail is in excellent shape. There are a few they didn't get to nearing Goose Lake but they can easily be walked around.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Good water through the whole canyon. Trail gets a little goofy right at this point. If you're headed CW / uphill on your right you'll see pink flagging tape that looks like it might be marking the trail. It's not. Trails goes to the left. It's a little faint in places and has a few blow downs but it's not too bad

name: S6 9.3 - JL (W3 100ydsN)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Caution. Blowdown exists along the upper portion of Goose Creek Trail (going CCW). Recommend using the forest road until 36.645, -105.44 and then returning to the trail by going XC downhill to the southeast.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Plenty of water in the lake and flowing out to Goose Creek. I took Goose Pond Rd over Goose Creek Trail due to the rain earlier in the day and expected in the evening.

sheetComments.author: Brian Tanzman
sheetComments.text: Flowing near trail, blowdowns have been cut except for a few close to Goose Lake

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Good flowing water all along creek

name: S6 9.6 - S6A START (W0)
details: Carsonite post in large cairn at top of switchbacks. End of Goose Creek Tr 65 at nexus of trails. Somewhat confusing on map, and only the main route ahead (W) is really visible on the ground here above treeline. This cairn also represents start of highly recommended Wheeler Peak Loop Alternate. Alt adds 18 mi to distance between Red River and San Cristobal resupply points. (Begin alt by heading S, effectively xc at first, then on visible singletrack tread heading into forest - Gold Creek Tr 64.)

name: S6 9.8 - S6A END (W0)
details: Signed trail junction. End of 18-mi Wheeler Peak Loop Alt. CW head N on Gold Hill Tr 64.

name: S6 13.7 - XT W2-3 0.4SE
details: Signed and cairned junction. Lobo peak tr straight; Columbine 71 N; Gavilan 60 S; Water likely along Gavilan Tr where it reaches drainage 0.4 SE and 500 feet below the ridgeline.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Flowing! A leaf helped to collect. Also ran into some local hikers and they called it Gavilan Spring so I think it’s reliable, just a ways down (and back up) from the trail

name: S6 20.1 - W2
details: San Cristobal Creek. Last access CW. Small flow here 6-20, more reliable below.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Here and below plenty of water.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Dry where you first cross it but about half a mile downhill it's flowing

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Just a trickle, could probably use a scoop/cup

name: S6 24.2 - W3
details: CW San Cristobal Creek last access. (CCW: flowing and frequent crossings for 3 miles.)

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: In addition to the larger campsites at 21.1 & 24.2, there is a small campsite for 1 or 2 tents at ~23.5 (36.6194, -105.5862)

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Plenty of water. Campsite  after last crossing. Look for path in field while heading towards trailhead. Cross river on makeshift bridge.

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Good flow the further down the creek you go.

name: S6 26.8 - W1
details: Acequia access approx here (such as if you'll be hanging out for the post office and aren't able to obtain water via the postmaster). Definitely treat irrigation water (avoid if at all possible).

sheetComments.author: Brian Tanzman
sheetComments.text:
I guess this is the ditch to the left? It was dry. I didn't see anything else.

The PO just ahead has a spigot out back accessible 24/7

name: S7 9.7 - W3
details: Arroyo Hondo creek access. Should be reliable and of better quality than the Rio Grande.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: The crossing was thigh deep. The Gaia track brings you to a good place to cross with easy entry and exit.

name: S7 9.7 - Dunn Bridge W3
details: John Dunn Bridge over Rio Grande

sheetComments.author: Mike Unger
sheetComments.text: Bridge is closed until mid Nov, 22. It is probably impassable if construction crews are present. Road on either side is also closed with a new barbed wire fence and “no trespassing “ sign. Hikers should consider the rio grande high water route during closure.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: The high water route is intended for clockwise hikers unable to ford the Rio west of Questa, relying on this bridged crossing in an emergency. So the high water route can’t serve its intended purpose right now with the bridged closure. Thinking a hiker reaching the bridge would need to attempt the ford here instead or backtrack if dangerous. For a clockwise hiker this might mean finding a ride to Taos Gorge bridge and resuming the route there.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Thanks Brett. Looks like I'll be fording or swimming at the original fording location.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: Kyle I think what it means is basically an extra required ford, first the usual one west of Questa, and then fording at Dunn Bridge after you exit the Columbine-Hondo and are on your way to Ranchos de Taos. However, what about crossing the bridge early AM or after 6 PM or whatever to avoid the construction crew.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Oct 2023. Bridge is open and there are vault toilets. Road up the west side of the canyon is accessible to foot traffic, but blocked to vehicles. I didn't check out the Black Rock Hot Springs just south on the east side.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Toilets, trash, shade. What else could you want?

name: S7 17.0 - TH W3
details: West Rim TH / Gorge rest area. Developed water (drinking fountains) 24/7. Restrooms closed after 5pm. Shade ramadas. Didn’t see vending. A popular spot, and you can follow the tourist throngs out onto the highway bridge for that iconic selfie. Route of NNML heads S from rest area lot onto signed West Rim Trail.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text:
Oct 2023. Water on. Note that the Earthship Visitor Center is just 1.5 miles NW of here if you are interested. 
https://earthship.com/visit/
To the north as you walk XC along private property you can see a number of their structures in the distance.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Fountain out of order but bottle filler is working. Taking a siesta at the shaded Ramada

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: 9/18/21: Coyote reports water off at gorge rest area but employee turned on a spigot for him. Will have to re-evaluate reliability (obv not W3)

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: Water was indeed off, talked to employee who said spigots are off for TBD amount of time as they need to be serviced due to leakage/water waste. Also stated new drinking fountains are being installed as we speak- finish date also TBD. Water in bathrooms is of course on, and potable according to same employee.

sheetComments.author: Mike Unger
sheetComments.text: Fountains and bottle fill station on. Toilets open.

sheetComments.author: Alan Tracey
sheetComments.text: Bottle fill station open. Toilets locked.

name: S7 28.8 - Taos Jct bridge W3
details: NM 567 Taos Junction Bridge over Rio Grande. Nearby vehicle-oriented Orilla Verde Campground as well as a boat launch / take-out with vault toilet.

sheetComments.author: Jac Mitchell
sheetComments.text: 9/21 all W3's have had water thus far due to recent heavy precipitation/also just being a generally reliable source, I imagine.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Water hydrant at campground is on

sheetComments.author: Brian Tanzman
sheetComments.text: Spigots on. Up ahead where the Picarus trail ends at the dirt road is a nice primitive campsite, $5. There's another one down the road a bit too. The self pay station is back towards the bridge though so it's kinda a pain to pick an open site and then backtrack. There's a camp host nearby so you need to pay. #1 is a nice quiet site next to the trailhead and surrounded by poison ivy and sumac but avoidable when setting up tent. I mention all this if you need a protected place to camp like we did in thunderstorms, not a lot of other options.

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman
sheetComments.text: Water spigots on at campground. I should've read Buck 30 and Kyle's comments before collecting from roadside spring which was awkward to collect from and flowing slowly.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Water at the developed campground. The primitive sites are great for camping in this beautiful area before/after Ranchos de Taos

name: S7 28.8 - TH (W2 0.25W)

sheetComments.author: Mike Unger

sheetComments.author: Mike Unger
sheetComments.text: Very small flow. Difficult to scoop.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Oct 2023. Just a trickle. Rio Pueblo at 29.3 miles was easily accessible as well.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Hardly anything. I passed over not thinking that it could be the spring

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman
sheetComments.text: 9/7 Just a trickle, would've been difficult to collect.

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer
sheetComments.text: Walked across a very solid beaver dam, didn’t have to get wet

name: S8 13.1 - W3 JR

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer

sheetComments.author: Andreas

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman
sheetComments.text: Beautiful, clear pooled spring flowing from the east side of FR 439, just after joining the road CCW. Better option than the river, what with the abundance of beaver dams 'round here.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Flowing here and at the other crossings. Pay attention because the junction is easy to miss (CW) and it's not a waypoint on the gaia / gpx. The road is subtle and very overgrown. Had to wade through the beaver pond but it was only knee high. I recommend getting water a little bit downstream where it's actually moving though
